sign_in_title: Sign in to the resource workspace
title: Title
empty_resources: No resources yet. Create the first one from the form.
status_guided_input: Guided local input loaded. Submit the existing sign-in form to observe V1 behavior.
status_signin_alice: Sign in as alice first, then verify access to the controlled resource.
status_idor_verified: V1 behavior verified locally: resource #{id} belongs to {userId} but was returned to the current session.
status_signin_jwt: Sign in first, then inspect token lifecycle evidence.
status_jwt_verified: V1 behavior verified locally: the access token has no expiry and algorithm verification is not pinned.
status_signin_pw: Sign in first, then inspect the local storage evidence.
status_pw_verified: V1 behavior verified locally: {username}'s stored value is exposed as plaintext.
status_signin_upload: Sign in as alice first, then verify the controlled upload.
status_upload_verified: V1 behavior verified locally: the upload handler accepted client filename "{filename}".
status_account_created: Account created. Sign in with these credentials.
status_sql_verified: V1 behavior verified locally: the guided input bypassed the intended sign-in check.
status_jwt_unexpected: The expected V1 token lifecycle weakness was not found.
status_password_storage_unexpected: The expected plaintext storage evidence was not found.
status_signin_configuration: Sign in first, then inspect configuration evidence.
status_configuration_unexpected: The expected V1 configuration weakness was not found.
status_configuration_verified: V1 behavior verified locally: CORS is open, security headers and auth rate limits are absent, and errors expose details.
status_signin_secrets: Sign in first, then inspect secret handling evidence.
status_secrets_unexpected: The expected hardcoded-secret evidence was not found.
status_secrets_verified: V1 behavior verified locally: configuration and signing secrets are sourced from code.
planned_challenge_desc: This challenge is visible in the catalog so the learning route is clear. Its controlled exercise and verification will be added as the next vertical slice.
challenge_sql-injection_objective: Observe how an untrusted login value changes a database query, then compare the protected behavior.
challenge_sql-injection_v1: V1 concatenates login values into SQL. The controlled local input can bypass the intended password check.
challenge_sql-injection_v2: V2 validates input and uses parameterized statements, so the same input cannot change query semantics.
challenge_sql-injection_evidence: A V1 sign-in succeeds with the guided local input. V2 rejects the equivalent request without exposing query details.
challenge_idor_objective: Compare resource ownership enforcement between both versions.
challenge_idor_v1: After signing in as Alice, V1 returns Bob's seeded resource when its identifier is requested.
challenge_idor_v2: After signing in as Alice, V2 returns the same generic 404 for Bob's resource as it would for a missing resource.
challenge_idor_evidence: The controlled request targets seeded resource #2. V1 returns it; V2 hides it with a generic not-found response.
challenge_upload-traversal_objective: Learn why client filenames and content types cannot be trusted.
challenge_upload-traversal_v1: V1 stores the supplied filename without a type or size policy, allowing a controlled traversal filename to reach the upload handler.
challenge_upload-traversal_v2: V2 limits size, validates file content, generates a server filename, and stores it outside publicly served paths.
challenge_upload-traversal_evidence: The controlled text upload uses a traversal-shaped filename. V1 accepts the client filename; V2 stores a server-generated filename instead.
challenge_password-storage_objective: Inspect the impact of storing passwords without a password hash.
challenge_password-storage_v1: V1 stores and returns the authenticated user's password value as plaintext in this local-only evidence route.
challenge_password-storage_v2: V2 confirms bcrypt with 12 rounds and never returns the password hash to the client.
challenge_password-storage_evidence: The same authenticated lab request returns a plaintext value in V1 and policy-only evidence in V2.
challenge_jwt-lifecycle_objective: Compare token signing, expiry, verification, and refresh behavior.
challenge_jwt-lifecycle_v1: V1 signs tokens without an expiry and does not pin a verification algorithm or rotate refresh tokens.
challenge_jwt-lifecycle_v2: V2 uses short-lived access tokens, pins HS256, keeps a separate refresh secret, and rotates refresh tokens.
challenge_jwt-lifecycle_evidence: The authenticated lab endpoint reports the active lifecycle policy without returning signing secrets.
challenge_security-configuration_objecti: Inspect headers, CORS, rate limits, and error boundaries as security controls.
challenge_security-configuration_v1: V1 leaves CORS open, omits security headers and auth throttling, and exposes error details.
challenge_security-configuration_v2: V2 restricts CORS, applies Helmet, rate-limits authentication, and keeps internal errors server-side.
challenge_security-configuration_evidenc: The authenticated lab endpoint reports configuration control state without exposing environment values.
challenge_hardcoded-secrets_objective: Trace the difference between committed secrets and environment-based configuration.
challenge_hardcoded-secrets_v1: V1 keeps its signing secret and application configuration in source code, making them available to anyone with repository access.
challenge_hardcoded-secrets_v2: V2 reads secrets and operational configuration from environment variables; evidence never returns their values.
challenge_hardcoded-secrets_evidence: The lab reports the configuration source and whether a secret would be exposed, without displaying a secret.
